title: Share of deaths from suicide
subtitle: Estimated share of deaths from suicide. Suicides are underreported in many countries due to social stigma and cultural or legal concerns. This data is based on modeling of global suicide patterns, and includes adjustments and extrapolations that aim to account for missing data and underreporting.
caption:
Share of deaths from suicide
Estimated share of deaths from suicide. Suicides are underreported in many countries due to social stigma and cultural or legal concerns. This data is based on modeling of global suicide patterns, and includes adjustments and extrapolations that aim to account for missing data and underreporting.

The line chart tracks the estimated share of deaths from suicide across seven countries from 1980 to 2023, with the vertical axis ranging from 0% to over 6%. South Korea displays the most volatile trajectory, beginning near 2.3% in 1980 and rising steadily to a peak above 6% around 2010 before falling to approximately 4.2% by 2023. Japan starts at roughly 3.1% in 1980, climbs to a high of about 3.3% in the late 1990s, and then declines to just under 2% by the end of the period. The United States line remains relatively flat between 1.5% and 1.8% for most of the timeline, ending slightly higher near 1.7%. Germany shows a gradual decrease from roughly 1.6% in 1980 to about 1.2% in 2023. The United Kingdom maintains a stable path near 1% throughout the four decades. Peru and Greece occupy the lowest bands; Peru rises from near 0.2% to roughly 0.7% by 2023, while Greece stays below 0.5% for the entire duration. The data excludes international aviation and relies on modeling to adjust for underreporting. The visual pattern shows South Korea experiencing a distinct rise and subsequent fall that exceeds the magnitude of change seen in any other country on the chart.
